coredata.dc:title: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe based on red emission carbon dots for nitrite detection in meat products
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Min
Zhang, Hui
Liu, Yingnan
Zhang, Yalan
Shang, Minghui
Wang, Li
Zhuang, Yuting
Lv, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrite (NO2 –) is widely present in the human environment and accurate, sensitive and selective detecting of nitrite is of vital significance for food safety and water quality. Herein, a novel red emission carbon dots (r-CDs) fluorescent probe was fabricated for dual-mode detection of nitrite, which was capable of both convenient colorimetric analysis and accurate fluorometric detection. When NO2 – is added to the rose-red r-CDs solution, NO2 – interacts with the amino groups which on the surface of r-CDs to form diazotized substance, resulting in that the colorimetric color of r-CDs solution realizes the transition from rose red to light purple, and the red fluorescence is gradually quenched. The detection limits of colorimetric and fluorescence for NO2 – were 0.193 μM and 0.149 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the dual-readout probe revealed satisfactory recovery and reliability when analyzing the concentration of NO2 – in ham and bacon samples..
coredata.dcterms:subject: Red emission
Carbon dots
Nitrite detection
Meat products

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay of NO2 – in actual meat products
Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of nitrite
Possible mechanism of this probe to NO2 –

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of r-CDs. (a) TEM image. The inset is the HRTEM image. (b) FT-IR spectrum (c) XPS survey; XPS spectra of O1s (d), N1s (e), C1s (f).

(a) The PL spectra of r-CDs at different excitation wavelengths. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum; fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. The insets show the optical photos of the r-CDs aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right).

(a) and (b) are the influence of the (r-CDs + NO2 –) system pH valve on the ratio of absorbance intensity (A386/A531) and fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0). (c) and (d) are the effects of the concentration of r-CDs on A386/A531 and (F0-F)/F0. (e) and (f) are the effects of the incubation temperature on A386/A531 and (F0-F)/F0. The final concentration of r-CDs and NO2 – are 15 mg mL−1 and 33.3 μM except Fig. 3(c) and (d), respectively. Error bars were estimated from three replicate measurements.

(a) and (c) are the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of r-CDs system in the presence of different concentrations of NO2 –, respectively. (b) and (c) are the calibration plot of the absorbance intensity ratio (A386/A531) and fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) versus the concentration of NO2 –, respectively. The concentration of r-CDs in all the above detection systems is 15 mg mL−1.

(a) Absorption intensity ratio (A386/A531) and (b) fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) of the r-CDs system containing different analytes. The concentration of NO2 – is 20 μM. The concentration of all other interfering substances was 150 μM. The final concentration of r-CDs in all the above detection systems is 15 mg mL−1.

(a) UV–vis absorption of NO2 –, fluorescence excitation and emission of r-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of r-CDs, r-CDs + NO2 – and NO2 –. (c) FT-IR spectra of NO2 –, r-CDs and r-CDs + NO2 –. (d) Fluorescence decay curve of r-CDs (450 nm) without NO2 – and with NO2 –.
Schematic diagram of probes based on red emitting carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorometric detection of nitrite.

head.ce:title: A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-readout probe based on red emission carbon dots for nitrite detection in meat products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling 712100, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrite (NO2 –) is widely present in the human environment and accurate, sensitive and selective detecting of nitrite is of vital significance for food safety and water quality. Herein, a novel red emission carbon dots (r-CDs) fluorescent probe was fabricated for dual-mode detection of nitrite, which was capable of both convenient colorimetric analysis and accurate fluorometric detection. When NO2 – is added to the rose-red r-CDs solution, NO2 – interacts with the amino groups which on the surface of r-CDs to form diazotized substance, resulting in that the colorimetric color of r-CDs solution realizes the transition from rose red to light purple, and the red fluorescence is gradually quenched. The detection limits of colorimetric and fluorescence for NO2 – were 0.193 μM and 0.149 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the dual-readout probe revealed satisfactory recovery and reliability when analyzing the concentration of NO2 – in ham and bacon samples..

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite (NO2 –), the most common nitrogen-containing compound in nature, is widely present in the human environment so that nitrite ion serves as an important indicator of inflammation, food safety and water quality (Nam et al., 2018; Sepahvand, Ghasemi, & Hosseini, 2021). Because of its excellent color protection and antiseptic function, nitrite is widely used in meat products as a color preservative and preservative (Hou, Fu, Ju, & Wu, 2020). As a color-protecting agent for meat products, nitrite can react with myoglobin in meat products to generate rose-colored nitrosyl myoglobin, which improves the color of meat to maintain a good appearance and also enhances the flavor of meat (Iacumin, Cattaneo, Zuccolo, Galanetto, Acquafredda, & Comi, 2019; Yang, Zhang, Xu, Wen, Zhang, & Zhang, 2021). Therefore, it is often added as a color retention agent in bacons and bacon in the food processing industry (Bonifacie, Promeyrat, Nassy, Gatellier, Sante-Lhoutellier, & Theron, 2021). Besides, nitrite has antiseptic properties and antioxidant capacity which could extend the shelf life of meat products (Berardo, De Maere, Stavropoulou, Rysman, Leroy, & De Smet, 2016). It also prevents the growth of Clostridium botulinum (Skibsted, 2011) and improves the safety of edible meat products (Karwowska & Kononiuk, 2020; Yoon, Nam, Lee, Kim, Lee, & Lee, 2021). However, it is worth noting that nitrites at high doses can still be very toxic and excessive ingestion of nitrites can lead to nitrite food poisoning, and long-term eat can even lead to esophageal and gastric cancers (Ding et al., 2018; Larsson & Wolk, 2006; Rong, et al., 2021; Viboonratanasri, Pabchanda, & Prompinit, 2018). Absorption of excessive nitrite by the human body will affect the operation of red blood cells, so that the blood cannot carry oxygen, the lips and fingertips will turn blue, commonly known as blue blood disease; Severe hypoxia in the brain, and even can be caused by respiratory failure and death (Yang, Zhang, Xu, Wen, Zhang, & Zhang, 2021; Zhang et al., 2017). Nitrite itself does not cause cancer, but below cook or other condition, the nitrite inside meat tastes can degrade reaction with amino acid, generate the N-nitrosamines that has strong carcinogenicity (Gan, Su, Chen, & Yang, 2020). Nitrite has become a member of the World Health Organization (WHO) list of carcinogens (Wang et al., 2021). Several countries have issued relevant regulations to control the use of nitrite in the food industry (Li, Ping, & Ying, 2019). Consequently, the accurate, sensitive and selective detection of nitrite content is of vital significance for food safety control and regulation. At present, there are various analytical methods for nitrite detection, such as electrochemistry, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) sensor, amperometric sensor (Ding, Cao, Liu, Lin, & Yang, 2021; Gill, Zajda, & Meyerhoff, 2019; Jian, Fu, Ji, Lin, Guo, & Ren, 2018; Li, Li, & Gao, 2019; Mako, Levenson, & Levine, 2020) and so on. Although these methods can obtain accurate and reliable results, they have limitations such as high detection costs, complicated and expensive equipment required, complex and time-consuming sample pre-treatment, and high requirements for the professional level of operators. Therefore, it is imperative to establish a simplicity, environmentally friendly, low cost, fast and accurate method for nitrite analysis.

Up to date, there are a considerable number of nitrite sensors based on fluorescent probes have been constructed for the detection of nitrite such as organic probes, semiconductor quantum dots, metal nanoclusters and carbon dots. Compared with traditional inorganic semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and carbon dots (CDs) are widely used because of their special superiority, including bright fluorescence, convenient synthesis, good biocompatibility and stability, easy surface functionalization and low cytotoxicity (Hu & Gao, 2020; Hu, Gao, & Luo, 2021; Hu, Gao, Yang, Chen, & Han, 2019; Zhai et al., 2018). For instance, blue-green fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with functional groups rich in amino groups, hydroxyl groups and sulfuric acid on the surface were synthesized by Deng and co-workers using p-amino benzene sulfonic acid. The sulfuric acid groups and aromatic primary amino groups on the surface of CDs interact with nitrite to cause fluorescence quenching (Deng, Qian, Zhou, & Niu, 2021). Rong et al. prepared green carbon dots (CDs) though one-pot hydrothermal method, the addition of NO2 – selectively induces the aggregation and caused fluorescence quenching of CDs under acidic conditions (Rong, et al., 2021). Sun et.al prepared N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with high sensitivity and high selectivity by one-step hydrothermal carbonization of chitosan, which can be used for nitrite detection (Sun, Zhang, Wang, Xiong, Zhao, & Xia, 2021). Even though these fluorescent probes have shown excellent results in the detection of nitrite, these probes can basically only achieve fluorescence single-mode detection. In contrast, the creation of a probe that can simultaneously realize the colorimetric/fluorescence dual-mode detection of nitrite provides more possibilities for the detection of nitrite in meat products.

Herein, we used neutral red and citric acid to synthesize red emission carbon dots(r-CDs) by hydrothermal method. This synthesis method is environmentally friendly, convenient, low-consumption, and time-saving. The synthesized r-CDs have the advantages of bright red emission fluorescence, easy surface functionalization good biocompatibility and good stability. After the addition of NO2 –, the interaction of NO2 – with the amino groups which on the surface of r-CDs leads to form diazotized substance, causing colorimetric and fluorescent color changes. As is clearly shown in Scheme 1 , after adding NO2 – to the r-CDs system, a new characteristic UV absorption peak appears in the UV absorption spectrum at 386 nm, and the fluorescence intensity at 602 nm decreases. At the same time, the colorimetric changes from rose red to lavender in the sun, accompanied by the gradual quenching of the red fluorescence of r-CDs. Based on this principle, a new type of colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode optical nanosensor for fast and accurate detection of NO2 – in meat products was successfully developed. It is worth mentioning that this detection system can realize the ratio colorimetric detection based on double peaks. Unlike most of the previous probes that can only achieve a single colorimetric or fluorescent detection of nitrite, this dual-mode probe based on red emission carbon dots we have constructed provides more possibilities for the determination of nitrite in food samples.

Sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), calcium chloride (CaCl2), aluminium chloride crystal (AlCl3), cupric chloride anhydrous (CuCl2), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4), sodium sulfite anhydrous (Na2SO3), sodium Carbonate (Na₂CO3), sodium hydrogen carbonate (NaHCO3), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium acetate (NaAc) and citric acid (CA) were purchased from Guangdong Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Serine (Ser), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), cysteine (Cys), Aspartic acid (Asp), lactose (Lac) and neutral red (NR) were furnished by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Potassium Iodide (KI) was supplied by Sinopharm Chemical ReagentCo., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium nitrite (NaNO₂) was provided by Tianli Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Unless otherwise expressly noted, all reagents listed above from commercial sources and used directly without further purification. The Ultrapure water we used throughout this study comes from the Millipore Milli-Q purification system (18.25 MΩ cm) in our laboratory.

The morphology of the prepared r-CDs was characterized by a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Hitachi, Japan). A Vetex 70 fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker Corp, Germany) was used for carried out FT-IR spectrum analysis to characterize the structure of r-CDs. To analyze the composition of r-CDs, we perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements on Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). The UV–vis absorption spectra were collected by 1.0 cm optical path length on a UV-2600 spectra photometer (Shimazu, Japan). All the fluorescence spectra measurements in this study were from a Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrometer (Horiba, USA). Fluorescence lifetime were measured using a steady-state/transient FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, England).

R-CDs were prepared based on the previous report, and with some minor changes (Gao, et al., 2018). In brief, 3.0 g CA and 4.2 mg NR were dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water with magnetic stirring at room temperature for two minutes to form a homogeneous mixed solution. Then transferred the solution to 30 mL Teflon stainless steel autoclave and further heated at 180 °C for 4.5 h. After cooling the resulted solution to room temperature, centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 10 min and then the supernatant was collected after being further filtered through a 0.22 µm pore diameter microporous membrane. Finally, the filtered solution was vacuum freeze-dried to obtain purified solid r-CDs. In subsequent experiments, the r-CDs were dissolved and diluted with ultrapure water.

A typical detection for nitrite was carried out according to the following procedures: prepared different concentrations of nitrite, added 100 μL different concentrations of nitrite standard solution to 500 μL of PBS (0.1 mM, pH 6.0) buffer solution in 1.5 mL centrifuge tube, then added 600 μL (30 mg/mL) of r-CDs and vortexed for 30 s. After incubating for 15 min in a water bath at 45 °C, the absorbance and fluorescence of samples were measured. Set the excitation wavelength to 510 nm for fluorescence spectrum measurement. Absorbance of the samples were recorded at the wavelength of 531 nm and 386 nm by a UV–vis spectrophotometer.

To examine the selectivity of the probe for NO2 –, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, AlCl3, CuCl2, FeCl3, FeCl2, MnCl2, Mg(NO3)2, ZnCl2, HgCl2, PbCl3, CrCl3, CdCl2, KI, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, NaNO3, NaAc, Ser, Gly, Lys, Cys, Asp and lactose were added to the detection system as interference to replace NO2 – standard solution. All experiments were repeated three times to ensure guarantee the stability and reliability of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of NO2 – in actual meat products
ce:section.ce:para: Meat products (including ham and bacon) were obtained from a local supermarket in Yangling (Shaanxi, China). The extraction of NO2 – in the ham and bacon samples was carried out in accordance with the procedures of the People’s Republic of China National Food Safety Standard (GB 5009.33–2010). First, weighed 5 g of sample for homogenate, placed it in a 150 mL conical flask with stopper, added 80 mL of water and 1 mL KOH solution (1 mol L-1), ultrasonically extracted 30 min, shook once every 5 min to keep the solid phase completely dispersed. Then put it in a water bath at 75 ℃ for 5 min, took it out and cooled to room temperature, transferred it quantitatively to a 100 mL volumetric flask, diluted with water to the mark and shook well. After filtering the solution through filter paper, a part of the solution was centrifuged and collected the supernatant to store in the refrigerator at 4 ℃ for later use.

R-CDs were synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method (Gao, et al., 2018) in this work. First, we compared with the UV absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum of r-CDs and neutral red (Figure S1). It shown that r-CDs and neutral red have similar optical properties. The morphologies, elemental compositions, and chemical bonding structures of r-CDs were characterized by TEM, FT-IR spectra, and XPS. The TEM image of r-CDs (Fig. 1 a) illustrated that r-CDs were uniform dispersed, the particle size distribution is between 1.4 and 4.2 nm, with the mean particle diameter is 2.71 nm (Figure S2). Furthermore, the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm in the HR-TEM image of r-CDs (inset in Fig. 1a) and presented the good crystallinity, which corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon (H. Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). The functional groups on the surface of r-CDs were further analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy and XPS. As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the characteristic peaks at 3497 cm−1, 1705 cm−1 and 1217 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the OH, CO and COC, indicating that there are abundant oxygen-containing groups in r-CDs. The two peaks at 1631 cm−1 and 1429 cm−1 indicate the existence of CC bond and CN bond, while the peak at 3296 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of NH, indicating the presence of amide groups (Qu et al., 2016). Furthermore, the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1c) showed that the r-CDs are composed of carbon (51.35%), nitrogen (0.92%) and oxygen elements (47.74%). It can be decomposed into three peaks at 532.3 eV, 533.1 eV and 533.8 eV (Fig. 1d) in the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum of r-CDs, which are related to the CO, CO, and C-OH/COC bands (Chen et al., 2020). The N 1 s spectrum could be divided into two components at 399.9 eV and 401.8 eV (Fig. 1e) indicate that the N mainly existed in the manner of CN and NH bonds. There are five obvious peaks at 289.3 eV, 289.1 eV, 287.1 eV, 285.6 eV, and 285.0 eV in the C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1f). According to previous reports, these five peaks correspond to CO, CO, CN, CC and CC key (Hai, Guo, Lin, Chen, & Wang, 2018; Song, Zhu, Song, Li, Du, & Lin, 2017; Xia, Zhao, Sun, Zheng, & Zhu, 2020). The above data is well consistent with the FT-IR results.

The optical properties of r-CDs were explored though UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 a, we provide the emission spectra of r-CDs at different excitation wavelengths and choose 510 nm as the optimal excitation for subsequent experiments. In Fig. 2b, the characteristic absorption peak of r-CDs is located at 531 nm (green line). Upon excitation with 510 nm radiation, the best emission wavelength of r-CDs is at 602 nm, and its excitation peak overlaps with the characteristic ultraviolet peak. The inset shows that the r-CDs solution is purple-red under sunlight, and emits red fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light. In addition, using Rhodamine B as the reference, the quantum yield (QY) of the obtained r-CDs was calculated as 39.3 % (Fig. S3).

To obtain the optimal signal response, a series of conditions were to investigate, such as pH, r-CDs concentration, reaction time and incubation temperature are not negligible. The above various experimental parameters were optimized to obtain optimal detection results.

In order to determine the optimal pH, we adjusted the pH of the buffer from 1.7 to 12.0, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 a and 3b. The absorption ratio and fluorescence quenching rate of r-CDs after the reaction with NO2 – showed an increasing trend in the pH range of the reaction system from 1.7 to 6.0. From 6.0 to 12.0, the absorption ratios and the fluorescence quenching rate decreases with the increase of pH. That is, the absorption ratio and fluorescence quenching rate reach the maximum when the system pH is 6.0. Thus, for the convenience of the experiments, choose pH 6.0 as the optimal pH for follow-up research.

In addition, the influence of the concentration of r-CDs on the performance of colorimetric and fluorescence analysis was also studied. The absorbance ratio changes and fluorescence quenching efficiency of nitrite under different r-CDs concentration systems were explored. To explore the influence of different concentrations of r-CDs (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 mg/mL) on the analytical performance of the detection system, the total volume of the detection system is set to 1.2 mL, while the volume of r-CDs is 600 μL. The best reaction concentration is depending on the colorimetric/fluorescence color change of the probe after adding nitrite into the detection system. As shown in Fig. 3c and 3d, the change trend of the fluorescence intensity ratio is consistent with the absorption intensity ratio. When the concentration of r-CDs is 15 mg/mL, the fluorescence quenching efficiency and ultraviolet absorbance ratio of nitrite to r-CDs is the highest. Therefore, the optimal concentration of r-CDs is 15 mg/mL.

The influence of incubation temperature on the detection system was also studied. In Fig. 3e and 3f, the fluorescence detection performance and ultraviolet analysis ability of the probe at different reaction temperatures are significantly different, and the probe performs best at 45 °C. Therefore, set to 45 °C as the optimal incubation temperature in subsequent experiments.

Finally, we investigated the influence of the reaction time. The variation in the absorbance ratio and fluorescence quenching efficiency of r-CDs with time after adding NO2 – were shown in Figure S4a and S4b. As time changes, the absorbance ratio and fluorescence quenching efficiency of the reaction system gradually become larger. After 15 min, the change of the absorbance ratio of the reaction system tends to be stable, which is consistent with the change of the fluorescence quenching rate of the reaction system. Therefore, the optimal reaction time between r-CDs and NO2 – is set to 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent detection of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: With the optimized conditions discussed above, the UV absorbance and fluorescence responses of the r-CDs system to different concentrations of NO2 – were examined to reflect the sensitivity of this probe. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increase of the concentration of NO2 –, the absorbance intensity of r-CDs at 531 nm gradually decreases, while a new characteristic peak appears at 386 nm and the absorbance intensity increases continuously. This change in absorption intensity is attributed to the possibility that NO2 – may interact with the amino group to generate diazotized substance. A good linear correlation (Fig. 4b) was obtained between the concentration of nitrite and the absorbance ratio (A386/A531) especially when the concentration of NO2 – was in the range of 0–60 μM (R2 = 0.999). The detection limit for NO2 – was calculated to be 0.193 μM according to the rule of 3δ/S (Xue et al., 2020). Thus, the r-CDs solution could serve as a resultful colorimetric probe for the detection of nitrite. In addition, the obvious change in the color from rose red to light purple can be observed. The corresponding color change diagram is shown in Figure S5a.

The fluorescence response of r-CDs fluorescent probe to different concentrations of nitrite were also studited. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity at 602 nm of r-CDs solution decreased with the concentration of NO2 – increased from 0 to 80 μM. The corresponding color change diagram is shown in Figure S5b. Fig. 4d showed that the fluorescence quenching rate ((F0-F)/F0) is positively correlated with the concentration of NO2 – in the range of 0–40 μM (R2 > 0.990), and the limit of detection for NO2 – was calculated to be 0.149 μM. The above results suggested that the r-CDs fluorescent probe was suitable for nitrite detection. The comparison of the proposed method with other analysis methods for the detection of NO2 – was given in Table S1. This dual-readout probe exhibited a better than or close to other analysis methods of the detection limit and linear range in Table S1, confirming the proposed strategy was demonstrated to be sensitive and highly reliable for the detection of NO2 –.

Besides sensitivity, selectivity is another crucial factor to assess the performance of our probe for target detection. To investigate the selectivity of the proposed assay strategy for NO2 –, the absorbance ratio and the fluorescence response to the interferences of various electrolytes and biological species were tested at a concentration of 150 μM. The analytical results were showed in Fig. 5 , including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, I-, Cl-, SO4 2-, SO3 2-, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, NO3 –, Ac-, Ser, Gly, Lys, Cys, Asp and lactose. It can be observed that only NO2 – exhibit a significant enhancing influence on the A386/A531 and (F0-F)/F0 of the detection system, while the other electrolytes and biological species do not cause any obvious changes in absorbance ratio and fluorescence quenching even when the concentration of these metal ions reached 7.5 times the NO2 – concentration. These results indicating that our detection system has good analytical capabilities and could be applied for the determination of nitrite in actual food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of this probe to NO2 –
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the high selective detection of r-CDs for NO2 –, the quenching mechanism was further explored. First, we compared the UV–Vis absorption of NO2 – and the excitation and emission peak of r-CDs (Fig. 6 a). As shown in the Fig. 6a, the characteristic ultraviolet–visible absorption peak of NO2 – appears in the range of 300–400 nm, and its absorption peak does not overlap with the excitation and emission peaks of r-CDs, indicating that the quenched fluorescence of CDs by NO2 – is not regulated by inner filter effect (IFE) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Gan, Su, Chen, & Yang, 2020). We further studied the changes of UV–vis absorption spectra, FT-IR spectra and fluorescence lifetime before and after the reaction of r-CDs with NO2 –. In Fig. 6b, the results show that r-CDs has a strong absorption peak at 531 nm, while NO2 – has an absorption peak at 353 nm. After r-CDs reacted with NO2 –, a new absorption peak appeared around 386 nm (this reflected the formation of diazo groups (−NN − )), while the absorption peak of r-CDs at 531 nm decreased and shifted to the right, accompanied by obvious color changes. Moreover, ζ-potentials and dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were performed on a Malvern ZEN3600 Zeta sizer Nano analyzer. As shown in Figure S6, the ζ-potentials of r-CDs and NO2 – were 7.46 and −3.61 mV, respectively, while the potential of r-CDs + NO2 – was 5.85 mV. It shows that the average particle size of r-CDs changed from 2.71 nm to 3.72 nm after the reaction with nitrite in Figure S7. These results indicated that new substances had been formed due to NO2 – interact with the groups on the surface of r-CDs (Liu et al., 2017; Mingcong et al., 2014).

The FT-IR spectra of r-CDs with or without NO2 – were also studied, in which a new peak was found at 1633 cm−1 corresponded to the -NN- bond (Fig. 6c) (Liu et al., 2020). We speculate that this may be due to the interaction between NO2 – and the amino group of r-CDs and form diazotized substance. The fluorescence decay curves of r-CDs before and after the addition of NO2 – were measured by a steady-state/transient FL spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). The decay curves are shown in Fig. 6d. As Table S2 summarized, after adding various NO2 – concentrations, the fluorescence lifetimes of r-CDs remained relatively stable, from 2.19 to 2.25 ns. According to the calculation of Stern-Volmer equation (Supplementary Information), which indicated that the quenching triggered by NO2 – is mainly due to static quenching. The absorption spectrum of r-CDs has changed while the fluorescence lifetime has almost no change, so the quenching of r-CDs can be attributed to static quenching.

To verify the feasibility of applying and the colorimetric/fluorescent probe for real samples, ham and bacon samples were tested for detection of NO2 –. First, the NO2 – content in ham and bacon samples is carried out in accordance with the method specified in the National Food Safety Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 5009.33–2016). Through calculation, the nitrite content of ham and bacon is 6.6 mg/Kg and 3.7 mg/Kg, respectively. Then, added nitrite standard solution to the sample and detected. The recovery tests are showed in Table S3, the concentration of NO2 -measured by the probe was basically the same as the spiked concentration and the recoveries of NO2 – in ham and bacon samples were fluctuated from 90.0% to 104.3%. The results demonstrate that the proposed probe has high accuracy and reliability for the real-time and rapid determination of NO2 – in meat products.

In summary, a simple, time-saving and economical probe we developed for the dual-mode determination of nitrite. In this detecting system, when NO2 – is added into the rose-red r-CDs solution, NO2 – interacts with the amino groups on the surface of r-CDs to form diazotized substance. At the same time, the r-CDs solution will undergo corresponding colorimetric and fluorescence changes. The colorimetric color realizes the transition from rose red to light purple, and the red fluorescence is gradually quenching. The absorption intensity ratio and the fluorescence quenching rate were linearly proportional to the concentration of NO2 – in the range of 0–60 μM and 0–40 μM, respectively. The colorimetric and fluorometric limits of detection for NO2 – were 0.193 μM and 0.149 μM, respectively. Remarkably, practical applications for meat products testing were successfully demonstrated. This method presented great advantages and convenience for sensitive and highly reliable detection of nitrite in food samples.

Min Yu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Hui Zhang: Validation, Visualization. Yingnan Liu: Validation, Visualization. Yalan Zhang: Validation, Visualization. Minghui Shang: . Li Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Yuting Zhuang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Xin Lv: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Chinese Universities Scientific Fund (No. 2452021123) and Talented Program (A279021724). Authors would like to thank the instrument shared platform of college of food science and engineering of Northwest A&F University, for the assistance in the FITR analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2021.131768.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Berardo et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of sodium ascorbate and sodium nitrite on protein and lipid oxidation in dry fermented sausages
Meat Science

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Berardo, A., De Maere, H., Stavropoulou, D. A., Rysman, T., Leroy, F., & De Smet, S. (2016). Effect of sodium ascorbate and sodium nitrite on protein and lipid oxidation in dry fermented sausages. Meat Science, 121(nov.), 359-364. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.meatsci.2016.07.003
